reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Strong when-to-use / when-not-to-use structure with concrete, decision-enabling content — not just circular definitions.
Comparison table covering all five packaging modes near the top gives the reader immediate orientation.
Code examples are realistic, copyable, and progressively built — basic → custom Dockerfile path → build args → batch/multi-container.

Correct source-grounded distinction that `entryPoint` is absent from BatchJob packaging (CustomDockerfileBjImagePackagingProps) but present for container workloads (CustomDockerfileCwImagePackagingProps), matched by the `<Info>` callout.
Security warning on buildArgs is concrete and actionable ('they are visible in the image layer history').
Best practices section is genuinely opinionated (non-root USER, pin base images, layer order) — reads like real production guidance.
FAQ mixes Stacktape-specific, AWS-service, and comparison questions well; 10 entries all earn their place.
Single `<ApiReference definitionName="CustomDockerfileCwImagePackagingProps" />` at the end, correct name from source.
No forbidden patterns detected: no raw YAML config, no StacktapeConfig type, no fenced TypeScript with defineConfig, no multi-command bash blocks.
problems: 'Caching behavior' section claims 'Stacktape calculates a checksum of the build context directory before each deployment. If the checksum matches the previously deployed image, the build is skipped entirely.' — this fact does not appear anywhere in the provided source file (deployment-artifacts.d.ts). It may be true, but it is ungrounded at this source level and could mislead readers if incorrect.

FAQ answer 'Can I build ARM/Graviton images?' claims 'Stacktape passes the `--platform linux/arm64` flag to `docker build` automatically' — not present in the provided source. This is a concrete Stacktape-specific behavioral claim without grounding.

The `<Info>` callout 'Your container must listen on port 80' is not grounded in the packaging source file, which says nothing about required listen ports. Port behavior is a web-service concern, not a packaging concern; stating it as an absolute fact on the packaging page without a source link could mislead users of private-service or worker-service resources that may use different ports.

ECR (Elastic Container Registry) is expanded on first use in the FAQ but not in the main body, where it appears in the introduction, 'How does Stacktape push the built image?' context, and 'Caching behavior' section. Readers with low AWS exposure may encounter 'ECR repository' several times in prose before reaching the FAQ explanation.
optionalImprovements: Ground or soften the 'Caching behavior' section: either cite a source, qualify with 'typically' / 'when supported', or remove the section until the behavior can be confirmed. Replacing with a sentence like 'Docker layer caching applies during builds — see [Docker docs] for details' avoids the ungrounded Stacktape-specific claim while still being useful.

Soften or remove the ARM/Graviton FAQ claim about automatic `--platform` flag injection. If confirmed from source, keep it; if not, replace with 'Container architecture is set at the compute resource level; consult [compute docs] for Graviton support' and remove the automatic-flag assertion.

Add a one-phrase ECR expansion on the first body-text mention: 'a managed ECR (Elastic Container Registry) repository' — the same wording the FAQ uses — so low-AWS readers aren't confused on the first encounter.

The port 80 `<Info>` callout should either link to the web-service or private-service resource page where the port requirement is documented, or be scoped explicitly: 'for web services and private services' (which it partially does) and note that worker services and batch jobs have no inbound traffic port requirement. This prevents a reader using `CustomDockerfilePackaging` on a `BatchJob` from thinking they must expose port 80.

The 'Applicable resources' section lists five resource types; consider a one-liner at the very top of the page (or in the opening paragraph) that states this packaging works with all container-based resources, so a reader scanning the page for their resource type sees it immediately rather than needing to find the section.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Clean packaging mode comparison table with links to all five alternatives — reader can orient quickly before committing to this page
Correctly distinguishes BatchJob (no `entryPoint`) from container workload resources (has `entryPoint`), with a grounded `<Info>` callout that matches the source types exactly
Realistic, self-contained code examples for every major configuration scenario: basic, custom Dockerfile path, buildArgs, command/entryPoint override, BatchJob, and MultiContainerWorkload
Security `<Warning>` for build-arg secrets is precise and actionable — tells the reader exactly what the risk is and what to do instead
FAQ covers all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS service mechanics, choosing/comparison) with 10 well-formed Q&As; no padding
Best practices section is genuinely useful, not filler — layer ordering, `.dockerignore`, base image pinning, and non-root user are all production-relevant
buildArgs Dockerfile counter-example (showing the corresponding ARG declarations) makes the two-part config pattern clear and copyable
No mandatory style violations: all Stacktape config examples use `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with TypeScript, no YAML blocks, no StacktapeConfig import, single `<ApiReference>`
problems: The 'Caching behavior' section claims 'Stacktape calculates a checksum of the build context directory before each deployment' for custom Dockerfile packaging. The provided source file only documents checksum-based caching for `stacktape-lambda-buildpack` and `stacktape-image-buildpack` (via their JSDoc). There is no such statement for `CustomDockerfileCwImagePackaging` in the provided source — this is an ungrounded Stacktape-specific behavioral claim.

The ARM/Graviton FAQ answer states 'Stacktape passes the `--platform linux/arm64` flag to `docker build` automatically.' The provided source file contains no mention of `--platform` flags, ARM architecture detection, or Graviton-specific build behavior. This is an invented implementation detail.

Minor: The `<Info>` callout 'Your container must listen on port 80' appears immediately under the basic example Dockerfile snippet, before the reader has seen batch job or worker service examples. The callout correctly scopes to 'web services and private services', but placing it as a direct follow-up to a generic Dockerfile example creates a moment of confusion for readers on this page for worker services or batch jobs. Low severity, but a precision issue.
optionalImprovements: Soften or qualify the 'Caching behavior' section. If checksum-based skip-rebuild is a real behavior for custom Dockerfile, cite or describe it more cautiously ('Stacktape may skip the build if the context has not changed since the last deploy'); if it cannot be grounded in the source, remove the section or reduce it to 'Docker layer caching applies during local builds; order layers by change frequency to maximize cache hits.' The Docker-layer-cache guidance is independently useful and source-neutral.

Rewrite the ARM/Graviton FAQ answer to remove the ungrounded `--platform` auto-injection claim. A safe rewrite: 'Container architecture is controlled at the resource level via compute settings. Verify that your base image supports the target architecture (multi-arch images like `node:20-alpine` work on both amd64 and arm64). For single-arch base images, use an explicit `--platform` in your `FROM` instruction.' This keeps the practical guidance without asserting behavior not in the source.

The `<Info>` port-80 callout could be tightened: 'For web services and private services, your container must listen on port 80 — Stacktape routes inbound traffic to this port.' Adding 'For web services and private services' as the grammatical subject (rather than an afterthought) makes the scope unambiguous on first read, before a reader processes the second sentence.

Consider adding a one-sentence note in the 'Using with batch jobs' section that batch jobs do not need to expose a port — they run to completion rather than serving traffic. This removes an implicit assumption readers carry over from the web service examples above.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and within the target length.
Stacktape config examples use class-based TypeScript inside `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with the correct `TypeScript` tab label.
The page names all five container packaging variants and gives a useful comparison table.
The page is practical and scannable, with clear sections for build context, Dockerfile path, build args, command overrides, batch jobs, and multi-container workloads.
problems: Several Stacktape-specific claims are not grounded in the provided source, including the default Dockerfile lookup behavior, mandatory port 80 behavior, checksum-based build skipping, Docker layer cache behavior as Stacktape behavior, automatic `--platform linux/arm64`, and ECR repository-per-resource wording.

The FAQ contains unsupported or risky AWS/Stacktape claims: no hard ECR image size limit, aim for under 500 MB, Docker Compose is not supported, private base images use local Docker credentials, and Stacktape surfaces Docker build output during deployment.

The page explains `buildArgs`, `command`, `entryPoint`, `dockerfilePath`, and `buildContextPath`, but some example tunables are under-explained: `cpu`, `memory`, `NODE_ENV`, `APP_VERSION`, and `dockerfilePath` values can read as recommended defaults rather than illustrative choices.

Some recommendations are too broad for the source. Examples include GPU drivers, distroless images, non-root users, pinned base image versions, and production security claims; these are generally good Docker advice but not supported by the provided Stacktape source.

The “Caching behavior” section is a major source-grounding risk because the only checksum-caching statement in the source is attached to Stacktape Lambda buildpack packaging, not custom Dockerfile container packaging.

The page has a good FAQ count, but the FAQ mixes source-grounded Stacktape behavior with general Docker/AWS operational advice without clearly separating what Stacktape documents from what Docker or AWS generally do.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or narrowly rewrite unsupported Stacktape-specific claims. In particular, fix the port 80 callout, checksum-based build skipping, automatic ARM build platform, Docker local credential behavior, Docker Compose unsupported claim, and ECR repository-per-resource wording unless additional source files support them.
Replace the “Caching behavior” section with source-grounded material or omit it. Do not attribute checksum-based rebuild skipping to custom Dockerfile packaging based on the provided source.

For `dockerfilePath`, say only that it is an optional path to the Dockerfile relative to `buildContextPath`; do not claim Stacktape defaults to `Dockerfile` at the context root unless a source states that default.

Revise FAQ answers so every Stacktape-specific behavior is grounded in the provided source. Keep general Docker/AWS advice only when clearly framed as Docker or AWS background, not Stacktape behavior.
optionalImprovements: Add a brief line near the first example clarifying that `cpu` and `memory` belong to the compute resource, not to `CustomDockerfilePackaging`.

Tighten the “When NOT to use” section by using source wording: Stacktape image buildpack supports JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Java, and Go; avoid unsupported “optimized image” claims unless sourced.
Consider adding a compact property table for the five `CustomDockerfilePackaging` properties: `buildContextPath`, `dockerfilePath`, `buildArgs`, `command`, and `entryPoint`.

In build-argument prose, explicitly state that `buildArgs` is a list of `{ argName, value }` entries passed to `docker build`; keep the secret warning as Docker best practice rather than a Stacktape-specific guarantee.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is generally aligned with the custom Dockerfile packaging type where it describes buildContextPath, dockerfilePath, buildArgs, command, entryPoint, and managed ECR upload. The main problems are unsupported Stacktape-specific behavior claims: default Dockerfile lookup, caching/checksum behavior, automatic ARM platform behavior, port routing, secrets injection mechanics, and batch-job entryPoint support. These claims are not grounded in the provided source file and several would change how a reader configures or operates the feature.

issues.statement: “If no dockerfilePath is specified, Stacktape looks for a file named Dockerfile in the build context directory.”
issues.reason: The source only marks dockerfilePath as optional and says what the path means when provided. It does not document a default filename or lookup behavior. This is a Stacktape-specific default claim that affects whether readers omit dockerfilePath.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording to the sourced shape: “The only required packaging property is buildContextPath. Set dockerfilePath when the Dockerfile is not at the path you want Stacktape to use.” If you need to mention Docker’s conventional default, phrase it as a Docker convention rather than a documented Stacktape default, or omit it.

issues.statement: “Stacktape calculates a checksum of the build context directory before each deployment. If the checksum matches the previously deployed image, the build is skipped entirely.” Also repeated in the FAQ: “Stacktape checksums the build context directory. If nothing changed since the last deploy, the build and push are skipped.”
issues.reason: The provided custom Dockerfile source documents that Stacktape builds the image and uploads it to ECR, but it does not document checksum-based skip behavior for custom Dockerfile container builds. The only checksum statement in the source applies to Stacktape Lambda buildpack packaging, not custom Dockerfile packaging.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the Stacktape checksum/skip claim for custom Dockerfile packaging unless another authoritative source is provided. Keep the Docker layer-cache advice if framed as Docker behavior.

evidence.quote: Packages are cached based on a checksum, so unchanged code is not re-packaged.

issues.statement: “When your resource uses Graviton/ARM instances, Stacktape passes the --platform linux/arm64 flag to docker build automatically.”
issues.reason: The provided source for CustomDockerfile packaging has no architecture, platform, Graviton, or automatic docker build platform behavior. This is a Stacktape-specific build behavior claim that would affect how readers author Dockerfiles and base images.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the automatic --platform claim. A cautious replacement would be: “Architecture is not configured on CustomDockerfilePackaging itself; configure compute architecture on the consuming resource if that resource supports it, and make sure the Dockerfile can build an image compatible with that runtime.”

issues.statement: “Your container must listen on port 80. Stacktape routes traffic to this port for web services and private services.”
issues.reason: The provided source file is limited to deployment artifact packaging and does not document container port requirements or routing behavior for web-service or private-service resources. The claim may be true elsewhere, but it is not grounded in the supplied source list.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the port-80 requirement from this packaging page unless the relevant web-service/private-service type source is supplied. If retained, scope it to the resource page and cite the resource behavior there.

issues.statement: “The entryPoint property is available for web-service, private-service, worker-service, and multi-container-workload. Batch jobs support command but not entryPoint.”
issues.reason: The source supports the packaging-type distinction: CustomDockerfileCwImagePackagingProps extends the batch-job packaging props and adds entryPoint, while CustomDockerfileBjImagePackagingProps has command only. However, the provided source does not map Cw packaging to specific resource names such as web-service, private-service, worker-service, and multi-container-workload. That resource applicability claim is not grounded in this source file.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the sourced packaging distinction and avoid naming resource mappings unless the resource type sources are available: “The container-workload packaging shape adds entryPoint. The batch-job packaging shape supports command but does not include entryPoint.”

issues.statement: “Secrets are injected as environment variables at container start time and are never written to the image.”
issues.reason: The source file documents buildArgs but does not document the $Secret directive, runtime environment injection, or whether secrets are never written to the image. The warning not to use buildArgs for secrets is reasonable Docker guidance, but the Stacktape-specific secret injection mechanics are not supported by the supplied source.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the build-argument warning and link to the secrets page without describing injection mechanics: “Do not use buildArgs for secrets because Docker build arguments can be exposed in image metadata or layers. Use runtime environment variables and Stacktape secrets instead; see /configuration/secrets.”

issues.statement: “Docker pulls base images using your local Docker credentials.”
issues.reason: The custom Dockerfile packaging source says Stacktape builds the image and uploads it to managed ECR, but it does not specify where the Docker build runs or which Docker credentials are used for private base images. This affects how users prepare private registry access.
issues.suggestedFix: Rephrase as a Docker prerequisite rather than documented Stacktape behavior: “If your Dockerfile uses a private base image, make sure the build environment used for deployment can pull from that registry.”

evidence.quote: * Stacktape builds the image and uploads it to a managed ECR repository.
* With `custom-dockerfile`, you provide a path to your Dockerfile and build context.

issues.statement: “Any publicly available or privately hosted Docker image works as a base.”
issues.reason: The provided custom Dockerfile source does not document base-image registry support. General Docker supports public and private base images, but the draft states it as an unconditional Stacktape-compatible claim without documenting credential requirements or build-environment constraints.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the answer: “Custom Dockerfile packaging can use whatever base image the Docker build environment can pull. Public images usually work directly; private base images require registry access in the build environment.”

positiveFindings: The page uses <CodeBlock intellisense> with a visible TypeScript tab for Stacktape configuration examples and does not hand-author YAML configuration.
The ApiReference definitionName matches an interface present in the provided source: CustomDockerfileCwImagePackagingProps.
The draft correctly documents the sourced properties buildContextPath, dockerfilePath, buildArgs, command, and entryPoint, including that command overrides CMD and entryPoint overrides ENTRYPOINT.
The draft acknowledges all five container packaging variants from the source and links only to routes present in the provided navigation index.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly aligned with the custom Dockerfile packaging type, but it makes several Stacktape-specific claims that are not grounded in the provided source. The highest-risk issues are unsupported defaults and deployment/runtime behavior that the source file does not document.

issues.statement: “If no `dockerfilePath` is specified, Stacktape looks for a file named `Dockerfile` in the build context directory.”
issues.reason: The source only marks `dockerfilePath` as optional and says what the path means when provided. It does not document the fallback filename or lookup behavior.
issues.suggestedFix: Write more narrowly: “`dockerfilePath` is optional. When you set it, the path is relative to `buildContextPath`.” Avoid naming the default Dockerfile lookup unless another source explicitly documents it.

issues.statement: “Stacktape calculates a checksum of the build context directory before each deployment. If the checksum matches the previously deployed image, the build is skipped entirely.” and FAQ: “No. Stacktape checksums the build context directory. If nothing changed since the last deploy, the build and push are skipped.”
issues.reason: The provided source documents checksum caching for Lambda buildpack packaging, but not for custom Dockerfile container packaging. The custom Dockerfile source only says Stacktape builds the image and uploads it to ECR.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the Stacktape checksum-skip claim from the custom Dockerfile page unless another source explicitly supports it. Keep the general Docker layer-cache advice if desired, framed as Docker behavior.

evidence.quote:
With `custom-dockerfile`, you provide a path to your Dockerfile and build context.
 * Stacktape builds the image and uploads it to a managed ECR repository.
Packages are cached based on a checksum, so unchanged code is not re-packaged.

issues.statement: “Your container must listen on port 80. Stacktape routes traffic to this port for web services and private services.”
issues.reason: The provided source is only the packaging type source and does not document port requirements or traffic routing for web-service/private-service containers. This is a Stacktape-specific runtime constraint and needs source support from the compute resource types or validators.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the callout from this packaging page, or replace it with a link to the web-service/private-service resource pages without stating a hard port rule unless the relevant source is provided.

issues.statement: “When your resource uses Graviton/ARM instances, Stacktape passes the `--platform linux/arm64` flag to `docker build` automatically.”
issues.reason: The custom Dockerfile packaging source does not mention Graviton, ARM, resource-level architecture, or automatic `docker build --platform` behavior. This claim would materially affect how readers write Dockerfiles and build images.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the automatic `--platform` claim. A cautious replacement could say that image architecture must match the resource’s compute architecture, if that is grounded by a compute-resource source.

issues.statement: “The Stacktape container buildpack auto-generates an optimized Dockerfile from your source code” and “the Stacktape container buildpack produces an optimized image with zero Dockerfile authoring.”
issues.reason: The source says the Stacktape image buildpack automatically bundles code and dependencies into an optimized container image. It does not say it auto-generates a Dockerfile.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the comparison to: “The Stacktape container buildpack automatically bundles your code and dependencies into an optimized container image, so you do not maintain a Dockerfile.”

evidence.quote: The `stacktape-image-buildpack` automatically bundles your code and dependencies into an optimized container image.

issues.statement: “Stacktape manages an ECR repository for each container resource in your stack.”
issues.reason: The source says the image is uploaded to a managed ECR repository, but it does not state that there is one repository per container resource.
issues.suggestedFix: Use the supported wording: “Stacktape uploads the built image to a managed ECR repository.” Avoid the per-resource repository topology unless another source documents it.

evidence.quote: Stacktape builds the image and uploads it to a managed ECR repository.

issues.statement: “Docker pulls base images using your local Docker credentials.”
issues.reason: The custom Dockerfile packaging source does not document how private base-image authentication is handled. The only private-registry credential support in this source is `repositoryCredentialsSecretArn`, and it is on prebuilt container image packaging, not custom Dockerfile packaging.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the local Docker credentials claim unless build implementation source supports it. If discussing private registries, state only that the Dockerfile may reference base images and that authentication behavior is not documented in this packaging type source.

issues.statement: “Custom Dockerfile packaging works with all container-based compute resources: Web service, Private service, Worker service, Multi-container workload, Batch job.”
issues.reason: The source distinguishes custom Dockerfile packaging props for batch jobs and container workloads, but it does not enumerate the exact resource classes that accept this packaging mode. The page instruction says container packaging pages apply to these resources, but the technical statement is not grounded in the provided source file itself.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the section if page-level instructions are treated as source for scope, but narrow the wording to “Container packaging pages apply to web-service, private-service, worker-service, multi-container-workload, and batch-job.” Avoid saying the type source proves compatibility with every listed resource unless the relevant resource prop unions are provided.

positiveFindings: The draft uses `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with a `TypeScript` tab for Stacktape configuration examples and avoids hand-authored YAML Stacktape config.
The `<ApiReference definitionName="CustomDockerfileCwImagePackagingProps" />` value matches a type present in the provided source.
The draft correctly documents the sourced custom Dockerfile properties: `buildContextPath`, optional `dockerfilePath`, `buildArgs`, `command`, and `entryPoint` for container-workload packaging.
The draft acknowledges all five container packaging variants listed in the source: Stacktape image buildpack, custom Dockerfile, prebuilt image, Nixpacks, and external buildpack.

verifierResults.summary: The draft covers the main CustomDockerfile packaging properties and names all five container packaging variants. I found no API-completeness, union-variant, or demonstrativeness issues that meet the medium/high threshold under the requested checks.
positiveFindings: The page documents every property in CustomDockerfileCwImagePackagingProps: buildContextPath, dockerfilePath, buildArgs, command, and entryPoint.
The page acknowledges all container packaging variants shown in the source: stacktape-image-buildpack, custom-dockerfile, prebuilt-image, nixpacks, and external-buildpack.

Each <CodeBlock intellisense> example demonstrates its section topic: basic build context, alternate Dockerfile path, build arguments, command/entryPoint overrides, BatchJob container packaging, and MultiContainerWorkload per-container packaging.

The draft correctly distinguishes BatchJob packaging shape from container workload packaging and notes that BatchJob supports command but not entryPoint, matching the source split between CustomDockerfileBjImagePackagingProps and CustomDockerfileCwImagePackagingProps.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Comparison table of all five container packaging modes appears early and clearly orients the reader before the deep dive — exactly what a first-timer needs before committing to one path.

Every Stacktape config example correctly uses `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} />` with `label: 'TypeScript'`, no fenced YAML, no plain-object StacktapeConfig style, and no fenced `typescript` blocks — zero forbidden-pattern violations.
The 'When to use' and 'When NOT to use' sections are correctly written as peer H2s with concrete, actionable criteria rather than circular definitions.
Practical depth sections (Docker layer caching, multi-stage builds, build arguments) go beyond mere configuration and explain the *why*, which is what a first-timer building in production needs.

The 10-question FAQ spans all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS-service questions like ECR/ECS behavior, and comparison questions like buildpack vs custom Dockerfile), and each answer is concise enough to be a featured snippet.

The `entryPoint`-vs-`command` distinction between the container-workload and batch-job variants is correctly noted and grounded in the source types (`CustomDockerfileCwImagePackagingProps` extends `CustomDockerfileBjImagePackagingProps` with `entryPoint`).
The `<ApiReference definitionName="CustomDockerfileCwImagePackagingProps" />` matches the correct Props type from the source and there is exactly one `<ApiReference>` on the page.
The build-arguments Warning callout about secrets in layer history is an important, non-obvious gotcha that a first-timer would otherwise discover the hard way.
problems: The 'Best practices' section uses bold text (`**Keep images small.**`, `**Use `.dockerignore`.**`, etc.) as de-facto sub-headers inside a single H2 blob. These are distinct, actionable recommendations that would be more scannable and navigable as H3 headings. A first-timer skimming the page cannot spot individual best practices without reading the whole section.

The first mention of 'ECR' (Elastic Container Registry) in the opening paragraph — 'uploads the resulting image to a managed ECR repository' — is unexplained. A reader with low AWS experience will not know what ECR is. The expansion 'managed Amazon ECR (Elastic Container Registry) repository' costs four words and removes the jargon barrier.

The debugging FAQ answer ('Run `docker build` locally… Stacktape surfaces the full Docker build output in the terminal during `stacktape deploy`') links to `/cli/deploy` but does not mention `stacktape debug:logs` or link to `/cli/debug-logs` for post-deploy log debugging, which is the more common debugging need once the image builds but the container misbehaves at runtime.

The `BatchJob` example uses `Bucket({})` with an empty argument object, which looks like an incomplete placeholder. For a copy-pasteable example, even `new Bucket({})` is fine syntactically but the surrounding context (a batch job that writes to a bucket) implies the bucket should have some purpose — at minimum the prose should note what the bucket is for so the reader understands the pattern.
optionalImprovements: Convert the 'Best practices' bold items into H3 sub-sections so each practice is individually navigable and appears in the page's table of contents.
Expand 'ECR' to 'Amazon ECR (Elastic Container Registry)' on first mention in the opening paragraph.
Add a sentence in the debugging FAQ answer pointing to `stacktape debug:logs` (linked to `/cli/debug-logs`) for runtime container failures after a successful build.

Add a note in the comparison table's 'Mode' column indicating which modes require Docker installed locally (custom-dockerfile, external-buildpack) vs. which do not (prebuilt-image, nixpacks) — this is a practical prerequisite a first-timer will hit immediately.

The 'Using with batch jobs' section states 'The batch job packaging variant includes `command` but not `entryPoint`' — this is also the correct takeaway from the 'Overriding CMD and ENTRYPOINT' section. Consider a brief cross-reference there instead of repeating it in the batch-job section to avoid the hint of redundancy.

The `dockerfilePath` prose says the path is 'relative to `buildContextPath`' but the example shows `dockerfilePath: './docker/Dockerfile.prod'` with `buildContextPath: './'`. Clarifying that `./docker/Dockerfile.prod` means the file is at `<project-root>/docker/Dockerfile.prod` would prevent confusion for readers new to Docker's context model.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: All source-grounded claims are accurate: buildContextPath, dockerfilePath, buildArgs (argName/value), command/entryPoint overrides, and the key BatchJob vs container workload distinction (BJ lacks entryPoint) all match the types exactly.
Packaging mode comparison table covers all five modes with links — satisfies union-type coverage discipline cleanly.
Concrete examples for three different resource types (WebService, BatchJob, MultiContainerWorkload) each with realistic configs — good per-resource depth.
The buildArgs secret warning is present, correctly placed, and accurate.
FAQ is comprehensive (10 entries) and spans all three required categories: Stacktape-specific, AWS-service behavior, and choosing/comparison questions.
Multi-stage builds and Docker layer caching sections are practical and include working Dockerfile examples — high signal for readers targeting production.
ApiReference uses `CustomDockerfileCwImagePackagingProps` which matches the type name in the source; single ApiReference at bottom follows convention.
No mandatory style violations: no YAML Stacktape config blocks, no StacktapeConfig type, no fenced TypeScript with defineConfig, no multiple commands in single bash block.
The CW vs BJ entryPoint distinction is mentioned both inline (Overriding CMD/ENTRYPOINT section) and in the FAQ — prevents a real gotcha from surprising users.
problems: The packaging comparison table is nested inside the `## When NOT to use` section, but it is a decision-support tool rather than a 'when not to use' statement. This makes the section feel structurally overloaded and slightly harms scannability because readers scanning for the table will look past it.

The `## Best practices` section uses bold headers inside prose paragraphs rather than a structured list or table. For a section the reader is likely scanning for a specific tip (e.g. 'how do I reduce image size?'), prose-with-bold is slower to navigate than a lightweight definition list.

The `dockerfilePath` description says the path is 'relative to buildContextPath' and the worked example shows `'./docker/Dockerfile.prod'` with `buildContextPath: './'`. The example is correct but the introductory sentence for the section says 'relative to your Stacktape config file' before correcting to 'relative to buildContextPath' one paragraph later. This is briefly confusing for readers setting an unusual buildContextPath.

The `## Overriding CMD and ENTRYPOINT` section ends with 'The batch job packaging variant supports `command` but does not include `entryPoint`.' This fact was already stated one sentence earlier in the same paragraph. Minor repetition within the same section.
optionalImprovements: Move the packaging mode comparison table out of `## When NOT to use` into its own lightweight subsection (e.g. a brief intro sentence before `## Basic example`) so the 'When NOT to use' section stays focused on decision guidance.

Convert `## Best practices` from bold-header prose into a two-column markdown table (Tip | Detail) or a definition list so readers can scan to the specific practice they need without reading every paragraph.

In the `## Dockerfile path` section, lead with 'The `dockerfilePath` property specifies the path to your Dockerfile relative to `buildContextPath`' (not relative to the config file) — consolidate the description into one sentence to remove the transient confusion.

The `## Basic example` uses `WebService` but the opening paragraph lists five supported resource types. A one-line note such as 'The same packaging config applies to PrivateService, WorkerService, MultiContainerWorkload, and BatchJob — see the dedicated sections below.' would help readers who are coming for one of the other types.

The `## FAQ` entry for 'How does Stacktape handle the built image?' is essentially already covered in the opening paragraph. Consider replacing it with a more distinct question, such as 'Does Stacktape cache Docker layers between deploys?' (build caching across deploy runs is a practical question many users have).

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and names `CustomDockerfilePackaging` clearly.
The page uses the required `CodeBlock intellisense` pattern for Stacktape configuration and avoids forbidden YAML/plain-object config forms.
The page covers the core workflow well: build context, Dockerfile path, build args, command/entrypoint overrides, multi-stage builds, batch jobs, and multi-container workloads.
The comparison table names all five container packaging modes, which satisfies union-type coverage at a high level.
FAQ is broad and useful, mixing Stacktape-specific questions, Docker/ECR behavior, debugging, and choosing between packaging modes.
problems: Several technical statements are not grounded in the provided source file or are stronger than the source supports, including local Docker layer caching behavior, `.dockerignore` performance claims, ECR image size claims, Fargate pull latency, non-root container advice, Alpine/distroless recommendations, and exact base-image examples.

The page says Stacktape “runs `docker build`”; the source only says Stacktape builds the image, uploads it to managed ECR, and that `buildArgs` are passed to the `docker build` command. This should be narrowed unless another source confirms the exact command execution model.

The `dockerfilePath` example uses `dockerfilePath: './docker/Dockerfile.prod'` while prose says the path is relative to `buildContextPath`. The leading `./` may be okay, but the surrounding explanation should make the relative path rule unambiguous.

The multi-container section says packaging modes can be mixed and names buildpack/prebuilt image, but the example only uses two custom Dockerfile containers. That weakens the decision support for the stated capability.

The `ApiReference` uses `CustomDockerfileCwImagePackagingProps`, but the page explicitly covers batch-job packaging too. Readers may miss that batch jobs use `CustomDockerfileBjImagePackagingProps`, where `entryPoint` is not available.

Some Docker best-practice sections read like general Docker guidance rather than Stacktape-grounded docs. They are useful, but they should be framed as Docker behavior or backed by additional sources.

The page includes specific tunable `resources.cpu` and `resources.memory` values in every config example without explaining that these belong to the workload resource, not packaging, and without grounding the supported values in the provided packaging source.
mandatoryFixes: Narrow or remove unsupported Stacktape-specific claims. In particular, avoid claiming task-definition updates, Fargate startup effects, ECR size limits, or Stacktape execution details unless those facts are present in the provided source files.

Clarify the two custom Dockerfile prop variants: container workloads use `CustomDockerfileCwImagePackagingProps` with `entryPoint`, while batch jobs use `CustomDockerfileBjImagePackagingProps` with `command` but no `entryPoint`. Keep the API reference strategy aligned with the page scope.

Add short prose anchors for `resources.cpu` and `resources.memory` values or remove them from examples if they are not necessary to demonstrate packaging. Do not make packaging examples appear to document compute sizing from a packaging source.
Revise the multi-container section so the example matches the prose about mixing packaging modes, or narrow the prose to the demonstrated use case.

Reframe Docker best practices as general Docker guidance and keep Stacktape-specific claims limited to the source-backed properties: `buildContextPath`, optional `dockerfilePath`, `buildArgs`, `command`, `entryPoint`, and managed ECR upload.
optionalImprovements: Move the five-mode packaging comparison closer to the top, before “When NOT to use,” so readers can choose between overlapping modes faster.
Add a compact properties table for the four source-backed custom Dockerfile knobs, separate from the API reference: property, applies to, purpose, and key caveat.
Use `dockerfilePath: 'docker/Dockerfile.prod'` in the example to make the “relative to buildContextPath” rule visually obvious.
Add one sentence near the batch-job example explaining why command override is common for jobs, while avoiding unsupported runtime behavior claims.
Replace “zero config” and “out of the box” phrasing with more concrete wording from the source, such as “automatically detects” or “requires no packaging configuration in most cases.”

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly grounded in the provided packaging type source and follows the required CodeBlock pattern. The main issues are a few Stacktape-specific statements that go beyond the source, plus one materially misleading Dockerfile example around build args.

issues.statement: “You do not need to create the repository, configure ECR access, or push the image manually. The resource's task definition is updated to reference the new image automatically.”
issues.reason: The source supports that Stacktape builds the image and uploads it to a managed ECR repository, but it does not state repository creation behavior, ECR access handling, manual push requirements, or task-definition update mechanics. Those details may be true in implementation, but they are not grounded in the provided source file.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the FAQ answer to the sourced behavior: “Stacktape builds the image and uploads it to a managed ECR repository. The packaging result is then used by the configured container resource.” Avoid claims about repository creation, ECR access configuration, and task definitions unless additional source is provided.

evidence.quote:
With `custom-dockerfile`, you provide a path to your Dockerfile and build context.
 * Stacktape builds the image and uploads it to a managed ECR repository.

issues.statement: The build-args Dockerfile example declares `ARG NODE_ENV` and `ARG APP_VERSION` before the `FROM` line, then uses both values after `FROM` in `ENV NODE_ENV=${NODE_ENV}` and `LABEL version=${APP_VERSION}`.
issues.reason: This is not a Stacktape type error, but the example is operationally misleading. Docker ARG values declared before `FROM` are only in scope for `FROM` unless redeclared in the build stage. Readers copying this example may not get the intended runtime `ENV` or label values.
issues.suggestedFix: Move the `ARG` declarations after `FROM`, or redeclare them after `FROM` before using them in `ENV` and `LABEL`.

evidence.quote: A list of arguments to pass to the `docker build` command.

issues.statement: “Secrets are injected as environment variables at container start and are not written to the image.”
issues.reason: The provided source file does not define the `environment` property, `$Secret()` behavior, or container secret injection mechanics. The warning that build args are unsuitable for secrets is reasonable Docker guidance, but the Stacktape-specific injection behavior is unsupported by the provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the actionable warning but narrow the Stacktape claim: “Do not use `buildArgs` for secrets because build arguments are build-time values. Use runtime configuration such as a resource `environment` value with `$Secret()` for sensitive values.”

evidence.quote: A list of arguments to pass to the `docker build` command.

issues.statement: “The only required packaging property is `buildContextPath`.”
issues.reason: For `CustomDockerfileBjImagePackagingProps` and `CustomDockerfileCwImagePackagingProps`, this is accurate within the provided interface. However, the page examples place packaging inside broader resources that also require other resource-level properties not shown in this source file. The sentence could be misread as a complete resource configuration statement rather than a packaging-only statement.
issues.suggestedFix: Clarify the scope: “Inside `CustomDockerfilePackaging`, the only required packaging property is `buildContextPath`.”

issues.statement: “Stacktape runs `docker build`...”
issues.reason: The source explicitly says Stacktape builds the image and says `buildArgs` are passed to the `docker build` command, so the claim is likely correct. The source does not state the exact command shape or whether Stacktape invokes Docker directly versus an equivalent build implementation.
issues.suggestedFix: Use slightly narrower wording: “Stacktape builds the image from the Dockerfile, passing `buildArgs` to the Docker build process.”

positiveFindings: All Stacktape configuration examples use `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with a `TypeScript` tab and `defineConfig`; there are no hand-authored YAML config examples or fenced Stacktape TypeScript config blocks.
The draft correctly documents `buildContextPath` as required, `dockerfilePath` as optional and relative to `buildContextPath`, and `buildArgs` as an array of `{ argName, value }` entries.
The draft correctly distinguishes the container-workload packaging variant, which has `entryPoint`, from the batch-job packaging variant, which has `command` but no `entryPoint`.
The draft acknowledges all five container packaging modes listed in the source: `stacktape-image-buildpack`, `custom-dockerfile`, `prebuilt-image`, `nixpacks`, and `external-buildpack`.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly consistent with the provided packaging type source and follows the required TypeScript CodeBlock pattern. The main factual risks are a few Stacktape-specific claims that go beyond the custom-Dockerfile source, especially around private registry base images, GPU examples, and prebuilt image pull behavior.

issues.statement: “Any Docker image works as a base — public images from Docker Hub, AWS ECR Public Gallery, or images from a private registry.”
issues.reason: The custom-Dockerfile source documents Dockerfile path, build context, build args, command, and entryPoint, but it does not document any private-registry authentication path for images used during a Docker build. The only private-registry credential property in the provided source is on prebuilt container image packaging, not custom Dockerfile packaging.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the statement to public base images and base images available to the build environment. Do not claim private registry base images work unless a source file documents how custom Dockerfile builds authenticate to that registry.

issues.statement: The draft recommends custom Dockerfile packaging for “GPU drivers” and names “NVIDIA CUDA” images as a use case.
issues.reason: The source supports using a custom Dockerfile for control over the container environment, but it does not document GPU-capable compute, GPU scheduling, or GPU hardware support for the Stacktape resources on this page. Mentioning GPU drivers and CUDA can mislead readers into thinking custom Dockerfile packaging enables GPU workloads.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the GPU examples, or replace them with source-grounded examples such as native libraries, system packages, multi-stage builds, and specific base images without implying GPU hardware support.

evidence.quote:
With `custom-dockerfile`, you provide a path to your Dockerfile and build context.
 * Stacktape builds the image and uploads it to a managed ECR repository.
 *
 * This gives you full control over the container environment and is ideal for complex setups.

issues.statement: “Stacktape skips the build step entirely and pulls the image at deploy time.”
issues.reason: The source says prebuilt-image packaging uses a reference to an existing image. It does not say Stacktape pulls the image during deploy. For container workloads, the safer source-grounded wording is that Stacktape references the existing image instead of building one.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the sentence to: “Stacktape skips the build step and configures the workload to use the existing image reference.”

evidence.quote:
With `prebuilt-image`, you provide a reference to an existing container image.
 * This can be a public image from Docker Hub or a private image from any container registry.
 *
 * For private registries, configure `repositoryCredentialsSecretArn` with credentials stored in AWS Secrets Manager.

issues.statement: “Stacktape surfaces the full Docker build output in the terminal during `stacktape deploy`.”
issues.reason: The provided source file defines packaging properties and says Stacktape builds and uploads the image, but it does not document CLI deploy output behavior or guarantee that the full Docker build output is surfaced.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove “full” and keep the debugging advice generic, or ground this claim in CLI/deploy source if available. For example: “Run `docker build` locally with the same build context path and Dockerfile to reproduce build errors outside Stacktape.”

positiveFindings: The draft uses `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]}>` with `label: 'TypeScript'` for Stacktape configuration examples and does not hand-author YAML.
The draft correctly documents the required `buildContextPath` property and the optional `dockerfilePath`, `buildArgs`, `command`, and `entryPoint` properties for the container-workload variant.

The draft correctly distinguishes the batch-job custom Dockerfile packaging variant from the container-workload variant: batch jobs have `command`, while `entryPoint` exists only on `CustomDockerfileCwImagePackagingProps`.
The draft acknowledges all five container packaging modes present in the source: Stacktape image buildpack, custom Dockerfile, prebuilt image, Nixpacks, and external buildpack.
`<ApiReference definitionName="CustomDockerfileCwImagePackagingProps" />` matches a definition present in the provided source file.

verifierResults.summary: The draft covers the primary `custom-dockerfile` packaging surface well: it documents `buildContextPath`, `dockerfilePath`, `buildArgs`, `command`, and the container-workload-only `entryPoint`; it also names all container packaging variants. I found no API completeness, union-variant, or code-example demonstrativeness issues that meet the reporting threshold.
positiveFindings: All properties on `CustomDockerfileCwImagePackagingProps` are mentioned in prose and demonstrated where useful: `buildContextPath`, `dockerfilePath`, `buildArgs`, `command`, and `entryPoint`.
The draft correctly distinguishes the batch job packaging variant from the container workload variant by saying batch jobs support `command` but not `entryPoint`.
The container packaging mode comparison names all five variants from the source: `stacktape-image-buildpack`, `custom-dockerfile`, `prebuilt-image`, `nixpacks`, and `external-buildpack`.

Every `<CodeBlock intellisense>` example is demonstrative for its section: Dockerfile path examples set `dockerfilePath`, build-argument examples set `buildArgs`, command override examples set `command` and `entryPoint`, and batch job / multi-container sections use the relevant resource shape.
